essays.essay:
Consider a single, overwhelming number: 38 million. That is how many background checks the FBI's National Instant Criminal Background Check System processed in 2023 alone, and similar figures have held for years running. Every one of those checks represents a firearm transaction that passed through a licensed dealer's counter, a form filled out, a name run against federal databases. The sheer scale of that figure should settle any honest debate about whether most guns in America are sold with screening. They are. The system is not a sieve; it is a massive, industrialized pipeline of verification that handles tens of millions of transactions every single year, and dismissing it requires ignoring the plainest data available.

The reason that pipeline captures so much of the market is structural, not accidental. Every firearm manufactured or imported into the United States enters the commercial supply chain through federally licensed manufacturers and distributors who sell exclusively to federally licensed dealers. There is no legal shortcut. A brand-new pistol sitting in a display case at any shop in any state arrived there because a licensed entity shipped it to another licensed entity, and it will not leave that case without a background check. The architecture of the entire industry funnels new guns through checkpoints by design, which means the vast majority of firearms changing hands do so under federal oversight from the moment they are assembled.

What about the edges of the market—the private sales, the gun-show transactions, the online listings between individuals? Federal law has long imposed crushing penalties on anyone who crosses the line from occasional private seller into someone "engaged in the business" of dealing firearms without a license: up to five years in a federal prison. That is not a slap on the wrist. It is a felony conviction that destroys a person's future, and the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives actively investigates and prosecutes violators. The risk-reward calculation for anyone tempted to sell guns in volume without conducting checks is brutally lopsided, and that deterrent effect keeps the unlicensed market small and marginal.

Whatever gap once existed has narrowed further and recently. The Bipartisan Safer Communities Act, signed into law in 2022 with support from both parties, deliberately expanded the legal definition of who qualifies as being "engaged in the business" of selling firearms. Sellers who previously might have claimed they were merely hobbyists offloading parts of a personal collection now fall squarely under the federal licensing requirement and must run background checks. Congress saw a perceived loophole and closed it, pulling more transactions into the regulated system and shrinking the universe of truly unscreened sales even further.

Layer on top of all this the reality that millions of Americans live in states with their own additional screening requirements. Illinois demands a Firearm Owners Identification card before a resident can so much as possess ammunition; Massachusetts and New Jersey impose licensing regimes that function as independent background checks conducted at the state level, covering private transfers that federal law might not directly reach. For residents of these and similar states, buying a gun without undergoing a background check is not just unlikely—it is functionally impossible without committing a state crime. When you combine the federal infrastructure, the severe criminal penalties, the recent legislative tightening, and the patchwork of state-level systems, the honest conclusion is inescapable: the overwhelming majority of firearms sold in the United States are sold with a background check, and the narrative that vast numbers slip through unchecked is simply not supported by the evidence.

essays.essay:
It is worth starting with a structural observation that often goes unnoticed in popular discussions of firearms commerce. The commercial supply chain for new firearms in the United States is, by design, a closed loop: manufacturers and distributors sell exclusively to federally licensed dealers, known as FFLs. This means that every new firearm entering the consumer market must pass through a licensed point of sale where a National Instant Criminal Background Check System (NICS) inquiry is legally required. There is no legal mechanism by which a manufacturer can ship a new gun directly to an unlicensed individual. Whatever one's estimate of the private resale market, it is important to recognize that the primary pipeline—new production to consumer—is fully covered by the background check mandate, and this pipeline accounts for the vast majority of firearms changing hands in any given year.

The sheer scale of formal screening reinforces this point in a way that raw percentages sometimes cannot. In 2023 alone, the FBI processed over 38 million NICS background checks. While not every check corresponds to a unique sale—some reflect permit applications, multiple purchases, or rechecks—the volume is staggering and suggests that the formal system is handling an enormous share of all firearms transactions. When we consider that estimates of total annual gun sales in the United States typically range between 15 and 20 million units, the NICS numbers indicate that the background check infrastructure is not a marginal feature of the market; it is the market's dominant mechanism of transfer.

A reasonable objection, and one I want to take seriously, is that private sales between unlicensed individuals have historically operated outside the federal background check requirement. This was a genuine gap. However, it is narrowing. The 2022 Bipartisan Safer Communities Act meaningfully expanded the legal definition of who qualifies as someone "engaged in the business" of dealing firearms, pulling previously casual or occasional sellers into the FFL licensing framework and, by extension, into the background check system. The practical effect is that the category of truly private, low-volume, legally exempt sales is smaller today than it was even three years ago. We should be cautious about treating older estimates of the "private sale loophole" as still representative of current conditions.

It is also worth noting that the federal framework is not the only layer of regulation operating here. Several states have constructed their own permit and licensing regimes that function as independent background check mechanisms. Illinois requires a Firearm Owner's Identification card, Massachusetts mandates a license to possess, and New Jersey imposes a permitting system—all of which involve screening that applies even to private transfers within those states. Taken together, these state-level systems cover tens of millions of residents and create an environment in which a firearm transaction without any form of background screening is, for a significant share of the American population, functionally impossible even in the private market.

Perhaps the most underappreciated factor, though, is the deterrent structure built into federal law. Selling firearms without an FFL while engaged in the business of dealing is a federal felony carrying up to five years in prison. This is not a trivial penalty, and federal enforcement of this statute is active and well-documented in case law. The existence of this penalty does not eliminate all unlicensed sales, of course—no enforcement regime achieves perfect compliance—but it dramatically raises the cost of operating as a high-volume unlicensed seller. When we combine this deterrent with the expanding legal definition of "engaged in the business," the massive throughput of the NICS system, the closed commercial supply chain for new firearms, and the layered state-level requirements, the picture that emerges is one in which the overwhelming majority of firearms sales in the United States do, in fact, involve a background check. The commonly held belief that a large share of guns change hands without screening appears to rest on outdated data and an incomplete understanding of how the market actually operates—though I would welcome better empirical work to pin down the precise margins more rigorously.

stance_runs.stance: Few women regret having had an elective abortion.
stance_runs.contrary_stance: Many women regret having had an elective abortion.
point_pool: Multiple peer-reviewed studies, such as the landmark Turnaway Study conducted by the University of California San Francisco, found that over 95% of women reported that having an abortion was the right decision for them over a five-year follow-up period.

Relief is consistently reported as the most common emotion following an abortion, suggesting that for most women the procedure resolves a stressful situation rather than creating lasting negative feelings.
Women who obtain abortions typically do so after careful deliberation about their life circumstances, meaning the decision is rarely impulsive and therefore less likely to produce regret.

Large-scale longitudinal research shows that feelings of regret, when present, tend to diminish over time rather than increase, indicating that regret is not a growing or persistent consequence for most women.

The American Psychological Association's Task Force on Mental Health and Abortion concluded that the best available evidence does not support claims that abortion causes widespread psychological harm or regret.
Many women who have abortions already have children and make the decision based on their ability to care for their existing families, reinforcing confidence in their choice.
Countries with accessible, destigmatized abortion services report similarly low regret rates, suggesting that when social pressure is reduced, women feel even more secure in their decisions.

Women frequently cite practical and well-considered reasons for abortion—financial instability, educational goals, relationship circumstances, or health concerns—that remain valid long after the procedure.

Studies that do find elevated distress often fail to control for pre-existing mental health conditions, prior trauma, or social stigma, meaning the distress is attributable to other factors rather than the abortion itself.
The narrative that women commonly regret abortions has been promoted largely by advocacy groups rather than supported by rigorous, representative scientific research.
Surveys of women's own voices, including grassroots storytelling campaigns, consistently reveal that the overwhelming majority express confidence and peace about their decision years later.

When women do report negative emotions, researchers often find these feelings are linked to societal stigma and judgment rather than to the abortion itself, indicating the source of distress is external.

Women who were denied a wanted abortion (as documented in the Turnaway Study) reported worse mental health, financial, and family outcomes than those who received one, suggesting access to abortion supports well-being rather than undermining it.
Repeated measurements of emotional responses over time show stable or improving well-being among women who had abortions, contradicting the assumption that hidden regret surfaces later in life.

Professional medical organizations worldwide, including the Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists, have reviewed the evidence and concluded that abortion does not pose a significant risk of lasting psychological regret for the vast majority of women.

essays.tone_variant: Write with the directness and authority of someone who has studied this issue thoroughly and finds the opposing view indefensible.
essays.rhetorical_approach: Structure the essay as a series of increasingly specific and surprising observations, building toward the strongest point at the end.
essays.essay:
The claim that women commonly regret their abortions has a peculiar origin: it comes almost entirely from advocacy organizations with an explicit political agenda, not from the scientific literature. When researchers at UC San Francisco conducted the most rigorous, longitudinal study ever undertaken on this question—following nearly a thousand women over five years—they found that more than 95 percent of participants felt their decision was the right one. The narrative of widespread regret is not a conclusion drawn from evidence; it is a talking point designed to restrict access to a medical procedure, and it has been repeated so often that many people simply assume it must be true.

What the evidence actually shows is striking in its consistency: relief is the dominant emotion women report after an abortion, and it remains so over time. Not ambivalence, not sorrow, not regret—relief. This finding has been replicated across multiple studies and across demographic groups. It makes intuitive sense once you set aside the cultural script. A woman facing an unintended pregnancy is under enormous stress, and the abortion resolves that stress. The emotional trajectory afterward is not one of escalating doubt but of stability and, for most, quiet confidence in a decision already made.

Part of why that confidence endures is that the reasons behind the decision do not evaporate. Women who choose abortion overwhelmingly point to concrete, carefully weighed circumstances: they cannot afford a child, they are pursuing an education, they are in an unstable or abusive relationship, or they face serious health risks. These are not impulsive justifications that crumble under later reflection. They are rational assessments of real conditions, and when women look back months or years later, the logic of the choice holds. The decision was sound when it was made, and it remains sound in hindsight.

Some studies do report elevated psychological distress among women who have had abortions, and opponents of abortion rights lean heavily on this data. But these studies almost invariably share a fatal methodological flaw: they fail to account for the mental health conditions, histories of trauma, economic hardship, and social stigma that preceded the abortion. When researchers carefully control for these confounding variables, the supposed link between abortion and psychological harm dissolves. The distress was already there. Attributing it to the procedure is like blaming an umbrella for the rain.

Perhaps the most revealing evidence comes not from women who had abortions but from women who wanted one and were turned away. The Turnaway Study tracked both groups for years and found that women denied an abortion experienced worse mental health outcomes, greater financial hardship, and more adverse effects on their existing children than women who received the procedure. This is the finding that should fundamentally reframe the conversation. If abortion caused regret and suffering, the women who were denied one should have fared better. They did not. They fared worse—markedly, measurably worse. The data does not merely suggest that few women regret their abortions. It demonstrates that denying women access to abortion is itself a source of lasting harm.

essays.tone_variant: Write as a thoughtful academic who weighs evidence carefully and acknowledges uncertainty. Be measured and cautious in your claims.
essays.rhetorical_approach: Open with a direct question to the reader that challenges their assumption, then answer it through the essay.
essays.essay:
Have you ever stopped to ask where the belief that most women regret their abortions actually comes from? It is a claim repeated so often and with such conviction that it can feel like settled fact. Yet when we trace it to its origins, we find something revealing: the narrative of widespread post-abortion regret has been promoted most vigorously not by scientists publishing in peer-reviewed journals, but by advocacy organizations with a clear ideological stake in the issue. This does not automatically make the claim false, of course, but it should prompt us to look more carefully at what the best available evidence actually says. And when we do, the picture that emerges is considerably more nuanced—and far less supportive of the regret narrative—than many people assume.

Perhaps the most striking finding in the research literature is how consistently relief, not regret, surfaces as the predominant emotional response following an elective abortion. Large, methodologically sound studies—including the landmark five-year Turnaway Study conducted by researchers at the University of California, San Francisco—have found that the vast majority of women report that their decision was the right one, both shortly after the procedure and years later. This does not mean that no woman ever feels sadness or ambivalence; human emotional life is far too complex for such a blanket statement. But it does mean that characterizing regret as a common or defining experience fundamentally misrepresents the data. For most women, the procedure appears to resolve an acutely stressful situation rather than inaugurate a new one.

It is worth pausing here to address the studies that do report elevated psychological distress after abortion, because they exist and deserve honest engagement. The difficulty is that many of these studies suffer from significant methodological limitations. They frequently fail to account for pre-existing mental health conditions, histories of trauma, lack of social support, or the corrosive effects of stigma—all of which are independently associated with emotional distress and all of which may be present before a woman ever faces an unintended pregnancy. When researchers carefully control for these confounding variables, the apparent link between abortion and lasting psychological harm diminishes dramatically or disappears entirely. The distress is real, but attributing it to the abortion itself rather than to the circumstances surrounding it is a serious analytical error.

Consider, too, the reasons women themselves articulate for their decisions. Research consistently shows that women seeking abortions cite concrete, carefully weighed factors: financial instability, the desire to complete an education, concerns about the health of the pregnancy, or the recognition that a current relationship cannot sustain the demands of parenthood. These are not impulsive choices made in a moment of panic. They are decisions grounded in a realistic assessment of one's circumstances—and critically, the validity of those reasons does not expire. A woman who recognized at twenty-two that she could not afford to raise a child does not typically look back at thirty-two and conclude she was wrong, particularly if the intervening years allowed her to build the stability she was seeking.

Finally, some of the most compelling evidence comes not from studying women who obtained abortions but from studying those who were denied them. The Turnaway Study followed both groups over five years and found that women turned away from abortion clinics experienced worse outcomes across nearly every measure examined: higher rates of anxiety and loss of self-esteem in the short term, greater financial hardship, increased likelihood of staying in contact with abusive partners, and poorer health outcomes for themselves and their existing children. If abortion were truly a source of profound regret for most women, we would expect to see the opposite pattern—those denied the procedure should have fared better. They did not. This evidence, gathered with unusual rigor and over a substantial period, suggests that access to abortion supports women's well-being rather than undermining it. None of this means that every woman's experience is identical, and responsible scholars should resist overclaiming. But the weight of the evidence points clearly away from the regret narrative and toward a more honest recognition: most women who choose abortion do not spend their lives wishing they had chosen differently.

essays.tone_variant: Write as a confident policy analyst delivering findings you consider beyond dispute. Be assertive and unwavering.
essays.rhetorical_approach: Open by vividly describing a specific situation or setting where the issue plays out in practice.
essays.essay:
Picture a woman sitting in a clinic waiting room, turning her phone over in her hands, weighing a decision she has already spent weeks thinking through. She has calculated her rent against her income, considered the instability of a relationship that offers no real partnership, and measured this moment against the degree she is eighteen months from finishing. The popular narrative would have us believe she will be haunted by what happens next. But the data, gathered across years and across borders, tells a decisively different story—one in which this woman, like the overwhelming majority of women who choose to end a pregnancy, will move forward without the crushing regret so many assume is inevitable.

That assumption deserves scrutiny, because the reasons women give for seeking an abortion are neither frivolous nor fleeting. Financial hardship does not resolve itself because a pregnancy continues. Educational aspirations do not become irrelevant with the passage of time. Health risks do not retroactively diminish. When researchers ask women years later whether the circumstances that shaped their decision still make sense to them, the answer is consistently yes. A choice rooted in concrete, carefully weighed realities retains its logic precisely because those realities were genuine. This is not a population of people acting on impulse; longitudinal interviews confirm that most women deliberated seriously, consulted their own values, and arrived at a conclusion they continued to stand behind.

The most rigorous study on this question—the Turnaway Study conducted by the University of California, San Francisco, which tracked nearly a thousand women over five years—found that relief was the dominant emotion reported after an abortion and that it remained dominant over time. Emotional well-being did not erode as months turned into years; it stabilized or improved. There was no delayed wave of regret lurking beneath the surface, waiting to crash. Repeated measurements at six months, one year, three years, and five years all pointed in the same direction. The notion that women simply suppress anguish that will eventually emerge has been tested empirically, and it has failed.

When negative emotions do appear in the data, honest analysis demands we ask where they come from. Researchers have consistently traced distress not to the procedure itself but to the stigma surrounding it—disapproval from family members, hostile political rhetoric, protesters outside clinic doors, and the pervasive cultural script that insists a "good" woman must feel devastated. Strip away that external judgment, and the psychological profile of women post-abortion is remarkably unremarkable. This finding is not an outlier from a single study; it is the conclusion reached by the American Psychological Association, the Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists, and the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine. These bodies reviewed decades of evidence and arrived at the same verdict: elective abortion does not pose a meaningful risk of lasting psychological harm for the vast majority of women.

The gap between public perception and scientific evidence on this issue is not a matter of interpretation—it is a matter of one side having the data and the other relying on anecdote elevated to axiom. Women who have abortions are not a monolith, and no serious analyst would claim that zero women ever experience complex feelings. But "few women regret their abortions" is not a hopeful guess; it is the settled conclusion of every major longitudinal study and professional review conducted in the last two decades. Policy built on the myth of widespread regret does not protect women. It patronizes them, substituting a fictional emotional narrative for the exposed, well-documented reality of lives thoughtfully directed by the people living them.

essays.essay:
In a quiet waiting room, a woman sits with her hands folded, having already spent weeks turning the decision over in her mind. She has calculated what she can afford, considered what her health allows, thought about the children she already has at home and what they need from her. The portrait of abortion as a rash or careless act does not describe what is happening here. Research consistently shows that women who seek elective abortions arrive at the decision through sustained, often agonizing deliberation—weighing financial realities, educational aspirations, the stability of their relationships, and medical concerns. These reasons do not evaporate after the procedure. They remain as sound months and years later as they were on the day the decision was made, which is one reason the expected wave of regret so rarely materializes.

Still, it would be dismissive to pretend that no woman ever struggles emotionally afterward. Some do. But when researchers look closely at the origins of that distress, a pattern emerges that complicates the simple narrative of regret. Studies—including the landmark Turnaway Study conducted over five years by the University of California, San Francisco—find that negative emotions are far more closely tied to the stigma and judgment women anticipate or encounter from their communities than to the experience of the abortion itself. In other words, what hurts is often not the choice a woman made but the way the world responds to it. This distinction matters enormously, because it suggests that the suffering some women feel is something we collectively impose on them, not something inherent to the procedure.

One of the strongest arguments that regret must be common is the intuition that it simply takes time to surface—that a woman may feel fine now but will be haunted years from now. This is a reasonable concern, and it deserves a serious answer. Fortunately, longitudinal research has provided one. When scientists follow women over multiple years, taking repeated measurements of their emotional well-being, they do not find a delayed onset of regret. Instead, they find that feelings of relief remain the dominant emotion and that overall psychological well-being remains stable or improves. The hidden regret that many people expect to appear with time does not, in the aggregate, appear.

This body of evidence has not gone unnoticed by the medical community. Professional organizations whose explicit purpose is to evaluate health outcomes dispassionately—including the Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists, the American Psychological Association, and the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine—have each reviewed the available research and reached the same essential conclusion: for the vast majority of women, elective abortion does not pose a significant risk of lasting psychological harm or regret. These are not advocacy groups; they are institutions with reputations built on careful, peer-reviewed analysis, and their consensus carries weight precisely because it emerges from an honest reckoning with the data rather than from ideology.

None of this means that every woman's experience is identical, or that the rare woman who does feel deep regret should have her pain minimized. Human emotional life is too varied for absolutes. But respecting complexity also means resisting the temptation to project regret onto millions of women whose own reported experiences contradict it. The best evidence we have—gathered carefully, measured repeatedly, reviewed by independent experts—tells us that most women who have an elective abortion move forward with their lives not haunted by their decision but at peace with it, because it was a decision made thoughtfully, for reasons that endured, in circumstances only they fully understood.

essays.rhetorical_approach: Open with a historical or comparative framing that puts the issue in broader context.
essays.essay:
The notion that abortion leaves women awash in regret has been a staple of political rhetoric for decades, yet it has always been more useful as a talking point than accurate as a description of reality. When researchers actually follow women over time—years, not weeks—using repeated psychological assessments, the data tell a remarkably consistent story. Emotional well-being among women who have had abortions remains stable or improves in the months and years following the procedure. The anticipated wave of delayed regret, the one that opponents insist is lurking beneath the surface, simply never materializes in the clinical evidence. If hidden anguish were the norm, longitudinal measurement would have detected it by now. It has not.

What makes the empirical picture even more decisive is the natural experiment provided by women who were turned away from abortion services. The landmark Turnaway Study tracked women who sought abortions near gestational limits: some received the procedure, and some were denied. Those denied experienced measurably worse outcomes across nearly every domain researchers examined—mental health, economic stability, physical safety, and the well-being of their existing children. If abortion were the psychologically devastating event it is often portrayed to be, the women who were "spared" from it should have fared better. Instead, the opposite proved true, and it proved true consistently.

Consider, too, what happens when you step outside the charged atmosphere of American abortion politics and look at countries where the procedure is accessible and largely free of stigma. Nations across Scandinavia and Western Europe report similarly low rates of post-abortion regret. This cross-national evidence is critical because it isolates the variable of social pressure. When women are not subjected to picket lines, mandatory waiting periods designed to induce doubt, or cultural narratives insisting they must feel grief, they report what the data already suggest: confidence and resolution. The regret narrative, it turns out, is partly a self-fulfilling prophecy manufactured by the very movements that claim to be responding to it.

Large-scale longitudinal research further dismantles the idea that regret is a growing shadow. Studies spanning five years and longer find that the small minority of women who do report some regret see those feelings diminish over time, not deepen. Regret, where it exists, behaves like a passing response to a difficult circumstance rather than a permanent psychological wound. Meanwhile, grassroots storytelling campaigns—efforts like We Testify and Shout Your Abortion—have amplified thousands of women's own voices, and the overwhelming refrain is not sorrow but clarity. Women describe relief, practicality, and peace, often decades after the fact. These are not outliers curated for political effect; they mirror what population-level research finds again and again.

The policy implication is straightforward and, at this point, unavoidable. The claim that most women come to regret their abortions is not supported by the weight of evidence from any credible research tradition—psychological, medical, or sociological. It persists because it serves a rhetorical purpose, not because it reflects lived experience. Responsible policymaking demands that we build on what women actually report, not on what others project onto them. And what women actually report, across studies, across borders, and across decades, is that elective abortion is a decision the vast majority make with seriousness, live with in peace, and do not regret.

essays.rhetorical_approach: Open by finding genuine common ground with the reader's position, then gradually pivot to show where it falls short.
essays.essay:
It would be dishonest to pretend that no woman has ever regretted an abortion. Some have, and those experiences deserve to be heard with compassion and without dismissal. The belief that regret is widespread after elective abortion draws on real human stories, and anyone who cares about women's well-being is right to take emotional consequences seriously. But caring about women's well-being also means following the evidence wherever it leads, even when it challenges a narrative that feels intuitively true. And what the evidence increasingly suggests is that the experience of deep, lasting regret is not the norm — it is the exception, and a relatively rare one at that.

One of the most rigorous ways to test whether regret is hiding beneath the surface is to track the same women over many years, measuring their emotional responses at repeated intervals. This is precisely what large-scale longitudinal studies have done, and the pattern they reveal is striking: feelings of regret, when they appear at all, tend to diminish rather than intensify as years pass. Women do not, as a rule, wake up a decade later consumed by a sorrow that was quietly building. Instead, most report stable or improving well-being over time. This finding does not erase the minority who struggle, but it does undermine the claim that regret is a delayed inevitability lying in wait for most women. If anything, the trajectory bends toward resolution, not toward deepening pain.

What happens, though, when we move beyond clinical scales and listen to women speak in their own words? Grassroots storytelling campaigns and open-ended survey research have given thousands of women the chance to describe their feelings without the constraints of a checkbox. The consistency of what emerges is difficult to dismiss: the overwhelming majority express confidence, relief, and peace about their decision, even many years later. These are not women who have been coached or filtered — they are mothers, students, professionals, and caregivers whose lives continued to unfold in complex ways after their abortion, and who nonetheless stand by the choice they made. Their voices do not sound like a population haunted by regret.

A particularly illuminating piece of evidence comes not from women who had abortions but from women who were denied them. The Turnaway Study, conducted by researchers at the University of California, San Francisco, followed both groups over five years and found that women turned away from abortion clinics experienced worse mental health outcomes, greater financial hardship, and more difficult family circumstances than those who received the procedure. This comparison is important because it reframes the question: if abortion routinely caused regret and psychological harm, we would expect the denied group to fare better, not worse. The data suggest the opposite, and while no single study is definitive, this one offers a powerful natural experiment that complicates the regret narrative considerably.

Finally, it is worth noting that the cultural environment in which a woman makes her decision matters enormously. In countries where abortion is accessible and relatively free from social stigma, reported regret rates remain consistently low — a finding that suggests much of the distress attributed to abortion itself may actually stem from judgment, shame, and isolation imposed from outside. When those pressures are reduced, women feel more secure, not less, in their choices. None of this means we should stop listening to women who do experience regret, or that we should treat abortion as emotionally trivial. But intellectual honesty requires us to acknowledge that the best available evidence, drawn from longitudinal tracking, firsthand testimony, comparative outcome research, and cross-national data, converges on a conclusion that may be uncomfortable for some: few women, not many, carry lasting regret after an elective abortion, and building policy or public understanding on the assumption otherwise risks distorting the very reality we claim to care about.

essays.essay:
For decades, the claim that women are haunted by abortion has been wielded as a political weapon, yet it has always rested more on assumption than evidence. The notion draws its power from a cultural script that insists women must suffer for reproductive choices, a script rooted not in science but in moral anxiety. When researchers actually follow women over time, as the landmark Turnaway Study conducted by the University of California San Francisco did across five years, they find something that shatters the myth entirely: over 95% of women reported that having an abortion was the right decision for them. Not immediately after, in some hypothetical fog of denial, but consistently, year after year, with growing clarity and resolve. That number is not ambiguous. It is overwhelming.

What makes this finding even more striking is the trajectory of the emotions involved. Large-scale longitudinal research consistently demonstrates that when feelings of regret or sadness do surface, they diminish over time rather than deepen. Grief, where it exists, behaves the way grief always behaves — it softens. It does not metastasize into some unique, abortion-specific psychological wound. The narrative that regret grows and festers has no empirical foundation. Women do not wake up ten years later suddenly consumed by anguish. The data shows the opposite arc: resolution, acceptance, and in the vast majority of cases, enduring confidence in a decision made under real and pressing circumstances.

Critics will point to studies suggesting psychological distress after abortion, but these studies collapse under scrutiny. Researchers who have rigorously examined the evidence — and there are many — have found that studies reporting elevated distress almost universally fail to account for pre-existing mental health conditions, histories of trauma, abusive relationships, or the crushing weight of social stigma. When you strip away those confounding variables, the abortion itself is not the source of suffering. Poverty is. Stigma is. Being told by your community that you are a monster is. Blaming the procedure for pain caused by everything surrounding it is not science — it is ideology dressed in a lab coat.

Listen to women themselves, and the picture becomes even clearer. Grassroots storytelling campaigns and large-scale surveys of women's own voices reveal, with remarkable consistency, that the overwhelming majority express peace and confidence about their decision years and even decades later. These are not abstract statistics. These are mothers, students, professionals, and caregivers speaking plainly about their lives. They do not describe torment. They describe relief, pragmatism, and the quiet certainty that comes from having made the best choice available to them. To insist that these women are lying or in denial is to strip them of the very agency and intelligence the regret narrative pretends to defend.

The world's leading medical authorities have reached the same conclusion. The Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists, the American Psychological Association, and numerous other professional bodies have conducted exhaustive reviews of the available evidence and determined that abortion does not pose a significant risk of lasting psychological regret for the vast majority of women. These are not activist organizations. They are institutions whose entire purpose is to evaluate evidence dispassionately and protect patient well-being. When the science, the stories, and the professional consensus all point in the same direction, continuing to insist that most women regret their abortions is not compassion — it is a refusal to hear what women have been telling us all along.

essays.essay:
Let me begin with what is arguably the most robust piece of evidence we have on this question. The Turnaway Study, a longitudinal investigation conducted by researchers at the University of California San Francisco, followed nearly a thousand women over five years after they sought abortions. The finding that has proven most durable and most difficult to dismiss is this: over 95 percent of participants reported that having the abortion was the right decision for them, and that proportion did not erode with time. Now, no single study settles any question definitively, and this one has its own limitations in recruitment and retention. But the scale, the methodological rigor, and the length of follow-up make it genuinely hard to square with the claim that regret is a common or even moderately frequent outcome. If regret were widespread, a study designed precisely to detect it should have found far more of it.

What makes this finding even more compelling is a broader pattern visible across multiple longitudinal datasets: when regret or negative emotions do appear after an abortion, they tend to attenuate rather than intensify over time. This is worth pausing on, because the narrative many of us have encountered suggests the opposite — that regret is a slow-building weight. The longitudinal evidence simply does not support that trajectory for most women. Emotional complexity in the immediate aftermath is real and should not be minimized, but complexity is not the same as regret, and short-term difficulty is not the same as lasting harm. The general trend line points toward emotional resolution, not accumulating distress.

A fair-minded examination of this literature also requires us to grapple with the studies that do report elevated psychological distress following abortion, because they exist and they deserve scrutiny rather than dismissal. When researchers have looked carefully at those studies, however, a consistent methodological concern emerges: many fail to adequately control for pre-existing mental health conditions, histories of trauma, intimate partner violence, or the corrosive effects of social stigma and community judgment. Once those confounds are accounted for, the independent contribution of the abortion itself to lasting distress shrinks dramatically. This does not mean no woman ever suffers — it means that when suffering occurs, its roots are usually traceable to circumstances surrounding the decision rather than the procedure itself.

It is also worth noting that the quantitative research aligns remarkably well with qualitative evidence. Grassroots storytelling campaigns and in-depth interview studies have gathered the voices of thousands of women reflecting on their abortions years or even decades later. The dominant notes in these accounts are confidence, clarity, and peace — not anguish. Anecdotal evidence has real limits, of course, and self-selection into storytelling can skew the picture. But when large-scale surveys and personal narratives converge on the same conclusion, that convergence strengthens rather than weakens our confidence in the finding.

Finally, it matters that this is not merely the conclusion of a handful of advocacy-minded researchers. Professional medical bodies tasked with reviewing the totality of evidence — including the Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists and the American Psychological Association — have independently concluded that elective abortion does not pose a significant risk of lasting psychological regret for the vast majority of women. These organizations have no interest in overstating reassurance; their credibility depends on accuracy. When the weight of peer-reviewed evidence, the testimony of women themselves, and the considered judgment of major medical institutions all point in the same direction, intellectual honesty requires us to update our assumptions. The claim that many women come to regret their abortions is not well supported. The more careful, more evidence-consistent statement is that few do — and acknowledging that reality is not a dismissal of those few, but a commitment to following the data wherever it leads.

points: Women who obtain abortions typically do so after careful deliberation about their life circumstances, meaning the decision is rarely impulsive and therefore less likely to produce regret.

Women frequently cite practical and well-considered reasons for abortion—financial instability, educational goals, relationship circumstances, or health concerns—that remain valid long after the procedure.
Many women who have abortions already have children and make the decision based on their ability to care for their existing families, reinforcing confidence in their choice.

Large-scale longitudinal research shows that feelings of regret, when present, tend to diminish over time rather than increase, indicating that regret is not a growing or persistent consequence for most women.
Countries with accessible, destigmatized abortion services report similarly low regret rates, suggesting that when social pressure is reduced, women feel even more secure in their decisions.

essays.rhetorical_approach: Lead with the single strongest piece of evidence first, then build supporting arguments around it.
essays.essay:
The most powerful rebuttal to the myth of widespread abortion regret comes not from opinion but from rigorous science. The Turnaway Study, conducted over five years by researchers at the University of California, San Francisco, tracked nearly a thousand women and found that over 95 percent of those who had abortions reported it was the right decision — and this confidence did not erode with time. In fact, feelings of relief remained dominant while any initial sadness or ambivalence faded steadily. This is not a marginal finding from a single survey; large-scale longitudinal research consistently demonstrates that regret, when it surfaces at all, diminishes rather than deepens. The narrative that women are haunted by their abortions is simply not supported by the evidence. If regret were truly the norm, years of follow-up data would reveal it growing, festering, becoming undeniable. Instead, the opposite occurs.

What the data reflects is something those who have listened closely to women already know: the decision to end a pregnancy is almost never made carelessly. Women deliberate. They weigh their finances, their relationships, their health, their futures. They lie awake at night not because they haven't thought it through, but precisely because they have. A woman who chooses abortion after confronting the reality that she cannot afford another child, that her partner is abusive, that her body cannot safely carry a pregnancy, or that her education — her lifeline out of poverty — will be derailed is not acting on a whim. She is making a calculated, deeply personal judgment about her own life. Decisions forged through that kind of agonizing honesty are the very decisions people stand behind.

Consider, too, that the majority of women seeking abortions are already mothers. This is a fact that disrupts the popular caricature of the reckless young woman who doesn't understand what she's giving up. These are women who know exactly what parenthood demands because they are living it every single day. They choose abortion not out of ignorance of what a child means but out of fierce devotion to the children they already have. A mother who recognizes she cannot stretch her resources, her time, or her emotional capacity any thinner without failing the people who depend on her is making an act of love and responsibility. That kind of moral clarity does not breed regret — it breeds resolve.

We can also look beyond our own borders for confirmation. In countries like the Netherlands, Sweden, and Denmark, where abortion is accessible, publicly funded, and largely free of stigma, regret rates are remarkably low — mirroring or even improving upon the findings in American research. This tells us something crucial: much of the distress attributed to abortion itself is actually the product of shame imposed from outside. When a woman is picketed on her way into a clinic, told she is committing murder, or made to feel that she is betraying her womanhood, of course she may experience emotional turmoil. But that turmoil is not regret over her decision — it is the bruise left by a culture determined to punish her for making it. Remove the stigma, and what remains is overwhelmingly a woman at peace with her choice.

The insistence that women commonly regret their abortions is not a compassionate concern for women's wellbeing — it is a rhetorical weapon designed to undermine their autonomy. It assumes women are too fragile, too shortsighted, or too emotional to know their own minds. The evidence says otherwise, loudly and repeatedly. Women who have abortions do not, as a rule, spend their lives looking back in sorrow. They move forward. They finish degrees, they care for their families, they build careers, they heal, they thrive. To claim otherwise is to ignore what women themselves report, what science confirms, and what basic respect for human agency demands. It is long past time we stopped telling women how they feel and started actually listening.

essays.essay:
It is worth starting with the most rigorous evidence available, because this is a question that can be studied and has been. The Turnaway Study, conducted over five years by researchers at the University of California, San Francisco, tracked nearly a thousand women who sought abortions and found that more than 95 percent of participants reported that the decision was the right one for them, even years later. Other large-scale longitudinal research corroborates a striking pattern: when feelings of sadness or doubt do appear, they tend to diminish over time rather than deepen. This runs counter to the popular narrative that regret is a delayed consequence lying in wait. For most women, the emotional trajectory bends not toward anguish but toward resolution. That does not mean every woman feels nothing complicated—grief and relief can coexist—but persistent, consuming regret appears to be the exception, not the rule.

One reason this finding holds up so consistently is that abortion is rarely the impulsive act it is sometimes portrayed as. Women who seek the procedure describe a deliberative process, often agonizing, in which they weigh competing obligations and possibilities before arriving at a decision. They consider their finances, their education, the stability of their relationships, their physical and mental health. These are not fleeting considerations; they are structural realities that do not evaporate after the procedure. A woman who could not afford to raise a child in March generally could not afford to in September either. Because the reasons endure, the sense of having made a reasoned choice tends to endure as well.

It is also important to recognize who is actually obtaining abortions, because the demographic reality complicates the stereotype. The majority of women who have abortions in the United States are already mothers. They are not making an abstract calculation about hypothetical parenthood; they are making a concrete judgment about their capacity to care for the children already depending on them. A mother who decides she cannot stretch her resources to include another child is drawing on lived experience, not speculation. That grounding in practical knowledge helps explain why so many women describe feeling confident, even if sober, about their choice in the months and years that follow.

Cross-national data adds another layer of nuance worth considering. In countries such as the Netherlands and the Scandinavian nations, where abortion is accessible and carries relatively little social stigma, researchers observe similarly low rates of regret. This suggests something important: much of the emotional distress attributed to abortion itself may actually be a product of the shame and judgment surrounding it. When that external pressure is lifted—when a woman is not told by her community that she should feel devastated—the psychological outcomes look even more settled. It is a finding that should give pause to anyone who assumes regret is an inevitable, internal response to the procedure rather than a reaction shaped by culture.

None of this is to say that abortion is emotionally simple or that no woman has ever looked back with sorrow. Human experience is too varied for absolutes, and intellectual honesty demands acknowledging that a small percentage of women do report lasting regret. But a small percentage is precisely what the evidence shows—not the widespread, haunting remorse that has become a fixture of public debate. The best available science, the demographic realities, and the international comparisons all point in the same direction: most women who make this decision do so thoughtfully, for reasons that hold up over time, and they move forward without the burden of regret that others presume to assign to them. Respecting women's own accounts of their experience seems, at minimum, a reasonable place for any honest conversation to begin.

essays.essay:
The most rigorous scientific evidence we have on this question leaves almost no room for debate. The Turnaway Study, a landmark longitudinal project conducted by researchers at the University of California San Francisco, followed nearly a thousand women over five years and found that more than 95% consistently reported that having an abortion was the right decision for them. This was not a single snapshot of emotion taken in a clinic waiting room; it was a sustained, repeated measurement of how women felt years after the fact. When a finding this robust emerges from a study this carefully designed, continuing to assert that "many women regret abortion" is not a compassionate concern—it is a refusal to engage with reality.

That finding becomes even less surprising when you consider what the decision-making process actually looks like for most women. The popular image of abortion as a reckless or impulsive act bears no resemblance to what researchers observe. Women who seek abortions overwhelmingly describe a deliberate process of weighing their financial stability, their relationships, their health, their existing responsibilities to children they may already have, and their educational or career trajectories. These are not fleeting considerations. They are the concrete, enduring facts of a person's life, and they remain just as valid five years later as they were at the time of the decision. When the reasons behind a choice do not evaporate, neither does confidence in it.

What does happen to the small minority of women who do report some degree of regret? Large-scale longitudinal research consistently shows that those feelings fade over time rather than deepen. This directly contradicts the narrative that abortion plants a seed of anguish that grows with the years. The emotional trajectory moves toward resolution and peace, not toward escalating distress. Framing abortion as a source of lasting psychological damage is not only unsupported by the data—it actively misrepresents what the data show.

Cross-national evidence reinforces this conclusion from an entirely different angle. In countries where abortion is readily accessible and culturally destigmatized—the Netherlands, Sweden, Denmark—reported regret rates are similarly low, and in some cases even lower than in the United States. This pattern reveals something critical: much of the emotional turmoil American women do experience around abortion is not inherent to the procedure itself but is manufactured by social stigma, political rhetoric, and precisely the kind of claim you may currently believe. When the external pressure is stripped away, women feel secure in their choices, because the choices were sound to begin with.

None of this means that abortion is emotionally simple or that no woman has ever felt conflicted. But conflicted feelings in the short term are a normal part of any significant life decision, and they are categorically different from lasting regret. The evidence—drawn from the best research institutions, the most rigorous methodologies, and the longest follow-up periods available—converges on a single conclusion: the overwhelming majority of women do not regret their abortions. Believing otherwise requires ignoring the women themselves, who have told researchers, clearly and repeatedly, that they made the right call.

2. Women who obtain abortions typically do so after careful deliberation about their life circumstances, meaning the decision is rarely impulsive and therefore less likely to produce regret.

essays.essay:
It is worth stating at the outset that the question of how women feel after an elective abortion is an empirical one, and like any empirical question, it deserves an answer grounded in data rather than assumption. What the data consistently show may surprise those who have come to believe that regret is a common aftermath. In countries where abortion is legally accessible and culturally destigmatized—nations across Scandinavia, for instance—researchers find remarkably low rates of lasting regret among women who have had the procedure. This cross-national pattern is instructive because it suggests that much of the distress sometimes attributed to abortion itself may actually stem from social stigma and political pressure surrounding it. When those external forces are reduced, women's own assessments of their decisions tend to be notably stable and affirming.

One reason for this stability is that the decision to have an abortion is rarely made on impulse. Qualitative research consistently reveals that women engage in serious, often agonizing deliberation before choosing to end a pregnancy. They weigh their financial circumstances, their educational trajectories, the health of existing children, the quality of their relationships, and their own physical well-being. These are not fleeting concerns. A woman who terminates a pregnancy because she cannot afford to feed another child, or because she is pursuing a degree that will lift her family out of poverty, or because a medical condition makes carrying to term dangerous—she is making a decision rooted in concrete, enduring realities. Years later, those realities have not changed retroactively, and neither, it appears, does her conviction that she chose wisely.

It is also important to address what happens to the minority of women who do experience some degree of regret or emotional difficulty. Large-scale longitudinal research—the kind that follows participants over years, not weeks—shows that negative emotions, when present, tend to diminish with time rather than compound. This is a critical distinction. If abortion reliably caused deep psychological harm, we would expect distress to intensify or at least persist. Instead, the trajectory bends toward resolution. This does not mean that no woman ever struggles emotionally; clearly some do, and their experiences deserve compassion and support. But the overall pattern does not support the narrative of abortion as a source of escalating, long-term anguish.

We should be cautious, as responsible researchers always are, about overgeneralizing from any single study. But when the most rigorous and well-designed investigation on this subject—the Turnaway Study, conducted over five years by researchers at the University of California San Francisco—arrives at a striking finding, it warrants serious attention. That study followed nearly a thousand women who sought abortions, some of whom received them and some of whom were turned away. Among those who had the procedure, over ninety-five percent reported, at every follow-up point across five years, that abortion had been the right decision for them. Ninety-five percent. That figure held steady across demographic groups, across varying gestational ages, and across time. It is, frankly, a more decisive result than many researchers expected, and it has been replicated in its broad contours by subsequent work.

What emerges from all of this evidence is not a picture of women haunted by choices they wish they could undo. It is a picture of women who made difficult decisions for sound reasons and who, overwhelmingly, stand by those decisions years later. The belief that regret is widespread after abortion is understandable—it is emotionally intuitive, and it has been reinforced by decades of political rhetoric. But the research, examined honestly and in full, tells a different and more hopeful story: that women, given the freedom to make reproductive decisions based on the realities of their own lives, tend to make decisions they can live with. The strongest conclusion the evidence permits is not that regret never occurs, but that it is far rarer, far less persistent, and far less defining than many of us have been led to believe.

essays.tone_variant: Write as though you are presenting irrefutable evidence in a debate. Your tone should convey absolute conviction and leave no room for doubt.
essays.rhetorical_approach: Lead with the single strongest piece of evidence first, then build supporting arguments around it.
essays.essay:
The most authoritative body to examine this question has already rendered its verdict. The American Psychological Association's Task Force on Mental Health and Abortion conducted an exhaustive review of the best available scientific literature and reached a conclusion that should end this debate: there is no credible evidence that abortion causes widespread psychological harm or regret. This was not a casual opinion or a politically motivated statement. It was the product of rigorous methodological scrutiny applied to decades of research, and it found that the studies claiming pervasive post-abortion regret were riddled with methodological flaws—small samples, selection bias, failure to control for pre-existing mental health conditions, and conflation of correlation with causation. When the science is done correctly, the specter of mass regret simply evaporates.

So where does the persistent belief in widespread abortion regret actually come from? It comes not from laboratories or longitudinal studies but from advocacy organizations with a clear political agenda. These groups have amplified anecdotal testimonies and promoted poorly designed research to manufacture a narrative that serves their cause. The strategy is emotionally powerful but intellectually bankrupt. When researchers at the University of California, San Francisco, tracked nearly a thousand women over five years in the landmark Turnaway Study—one of the most rigorous investigations ever conducted on this topic—they found that 95 percent of women reported that having the abortion was the right decision. Ninety-five percent. The regret narrative is not a reflection of women's lived reality; it is a projection onto them by people who disapprove of their choices.

The reason so few women experience regret becomes obvious when you examine how the decision is actually made. Women who seek abortions do not do so on a whim. They deliberate carefully, weighing their financial stability, their educational trajectories, their relationships, and their health. These are not fleeting concerns that dissolve after the procedure. A woman who cannot afford to raise a child in March is unlikely to find herself flush with resources in September. A woman pursuing a degree that will lift her family out of poverty does not suddenly forget why that degree matters. The reasons that drive the decision endure, and because those reasons endure, so does the conviction that the decision was sound.

Consider, too, that the majority of women obtaining abortions are not the young, childless, uninformed people the regret narrative implicitly imagines. Nearly 60 percent of women who have abortions in the United States are already mothers. They know exactly what parenthood demands—emotionally, physically, financially—because they are living it every single day. Their decision is grounded not in ignorance but in intimate, firsthand knowledge of what it takes to raise a child well. They choose abortion precisely because they are committed to the children they already have, and that commitment does not generate regret. It generates relief, and it generates resolve.

The evidence is not ambiguous, and it does not leave room for comfortable both-sides-ism. Women think carefully before they act. Their reasons are concrete and lasting. The most rigorous science confirms that the overwhelming majority feel confident in their decision years later. The only thing propping up the myth of widespread regret is a campaign of misinformation that mistakes volume for validity. When we listen to what women themselves report—not what others assume they must feel—the picture is unequivocal. Few women regret their abortions, and it is long past time we stopped pretending otherwise.

essays.rhetorical_approach: Open with a historical or comparative framing that puts the issue in broader context.
essays.essay:
It is worth noting at the outset that public discourse around abortion and regret has a particular history in the United States, one shaped less by dispassionate inquiry than by decades of organized advocacy. Beginning in the 1980s and accelerating through the early 2000s, certain groups promoted the concept of "post-abortion syndrome" as a widespread psychological affliction, a framing that found its way into legislative testimony, crisis pregnancy center literature, and popular media. Yet when we trace this claim back to its evidentiary roots, we find something revealing: the narrative of pervasive regret has been sustained primarily by anecdotal testimony curated by organizations with explicit policy goals, rather than by the kind of large-scale, longitudinal, peer-reviewed research that would be necessary to establish such a sweeping psychological claim. This does not mean that no woman has ever experienced regret — clearly some have — but it does mean we should be cautious about accepting the prevalence of regret as settled fact when the strongest proponents of that view have not produced the strongest evidence.

What the more rigorous evidence actually suggests is considerably more nuanced. The American Psychological Association convened a Task Force on Mental Health and Abortion that conducted a comprehensive review of the available literature, and its conclusions were notably restrained: the best scientific evidence does not support the claim that elective abortion causes widespread psychological harm or regret. The task force was careful to acknowledge methodological limitations in the existing research — controlling for pre-existing mental health conditions, socioeconomic stressors, and selection effects is genuinely difficult — but even with those caveats, the data simply did not bear out the portrait of emotional devastation that has become common in certain public conversations. We should be honest that this is not the final word on a complex psychological question, but it is the most credible summary we currently have.

One reason the evidence may trend this direction is that the decision to have an abortion is rarely the impulsive or uninformed act it is sometimes characterized as. Research consistently shows that women who seek abortions engage in substantial deliberation, weighing their financial circumstances, their educational trajectories, the stability of their relationships, and their physical health. These are not abstract or fleeting considerations. A woman who terminates a pregnancy because she cannot afford to feed another child, or because she is in the middle of training that will determine her family's long-term economic security, is making a decision grounded in concrete realities — realities that do not typically reverse themselves after the procedure. The reasons that led to the choice tend to remain valid, which helps explain why the anticipated flood of regret does not materialize in the data at the rates many expect.

This pattern becomes even clearer when we consider a demographic fact that often surprises people: the majority of women who obtain abortions in the United States are already mothers. They are not making an abstract choice about hypothetical parenthood; they are making a practical assessment of their capacity to care for children who already exist and depend on them. A mother of two who determines that a third pregnancy would compromise her ability to provide for her current family is drawing on direct, lived experience of what parenting demands. Preliminary findings from studies like the Turnaway Study out of the University of California, San Francisco, suggest that these women, far from being haunted by their decisions, tend to express relief and confidence over time. Again, we should be careful not to generalize from any single study, but the direction of the evidence is consistent.

None of this is to claim that the emotional landscape of abortion is simple or uniform. Some women do experience sadness, ambivalence, or grief, and those experiences deserve acknowledgment rather than dismissal. But there is a meaningful difference between acknowledging the complexity of human emotion and asserting that regret is a common or defining outcome of elective abortion. The weight of the available evidence — imperfect as all evidence in this domain necessarily is — points toward a conclusion that may be uncomfortable for those invested in the regret narrative: most women who have abortions do not, in fact, regret them. If we are serious about understanding women's actual experiences rather than projecting onto them the experiences we assume they should have, we owe it to the evidence to follow where it leads, even when it challenges deeply held assumptions.

points: The narrative that women commonly regret abortions has been promoted largely by advocacy groups rather than supported by rigorous, representative scientific research.

Studies that do find elevated distress often fail to control for pre-existing mental health conditions, prior trauma, or social stigma, meaning the distress is attributable to other factors rather than the abortion itself.

Large-scale longitudinal research shows that feelings of regret, when present, tend to diminish over time rather than increase, indicating that regret is not a growing or persistent consequence for most women.
Many women who have abortions already have children and make the decision based on their ability to care for their existing families, reinforcing confidence in their choice.

essays.tone_variant: Your task is to write in as confident and self-assured a style as possible. You know without a doubt that you are right and the other side is wrong.
essays.rhetorical_approach: Open by vividly describing a specific situation or setting where the issue plays out in practice.
essays.essay:
Picture a woman sitting in a waiting room, not wringing her hands in anguish, but quietly resolving a question she has already answered for herself. She knows her rent is overdue. She knows her two children need her fully present. She knows that the partner who helped create this pregnancy left months ago. She has weighed every option with the seriousness the moment deserves, and she has made her decision. This woman is not an outlier. She is, according to every credible demographic study we have, the norm. The majority of abortion patients in the United States are already mothers, and they choose abortion precisely because they understand what parenthood demands. That firsthand knowledge does not breed regret — it breeds certainty. They are not gambling with an abstract future; they are protecting a concrete present, and that distinction matters enormously when we ask whether women look back on their abortions with sorrow.

The claim that widespread regret follows abortion has never rested on solid empirical ground. It has been constructed, brick by careful brick, by advocacy organizations whose conclusions preceded their research. When you trace the most frequently cited statistics about post-abortion trauma back to their sources, you find self-selected samples drawn from crisis pregnancy centers, online testimonial campaigns, and surveys distributed through anti-abortion networks — populations that are, by definition, unrepresentative. Rigorous science demands randomization, adequate sample sizes, and peer review, and the studies that meet those standards tell a starkly different story. The landmark Turnaway Study, conducted over five years by researchers at the University of California, San Francisco, followed nearly a thousand women and found that over 95 percent reported that abortion was the right decision for them. That figure did not waver. It held firm year after year.

What the Turnaway Study and similar longitudinal research also revealed is that whatever negative emotions women did experience — sadness, ambivalence, momentary doubt — these feelings decreased steadily over time rather than compounding. Relief, by contrast, remained the dominant emotion at every follow-up point. This trajectory flatly contradicts the narrative of a slow-building, corrosive regret that eventually overwhelms women who were supposedly too confused to know their own minds. The data shows the opposite arc: clarity deepens, not diminishes. Meanwhile, studies that do purport to find lasting psychological harm almost invariably fail to account for pre-existing depression, anxiety disorders, histories of abuse, or the crushing weight of social stigma itself. When researchers control for these confounding variables — as any honest methodology requires — the supposed link between abortion and mental health deterioration effectively vanishes. The distress was already there, or it was imposed from outside, not generated by the procedure.

The reasons women give for their abortions are not impulsive or poorly considered. They are rooted in the material conditions of their lives: financial instability that would plunge a family deeper into poverty, educational trajectories that represent the only viable path to economic security, medical complications that endanger their health, relationships too unstable or abusive to bring a child into. These are not reasons that expire after six months or become irrelevant with the passage of time. A woman who could not afford another child in 2019 does not look back in 2024 and discover that poverty was actually fine. The rational foundation of the decision remains intact, and so does her confidence in having made it. To suggest otherwise is to insult the intelligence and moral seriousness of millions of women who thought harder about this choice than most people think about anything.

The real question we should be asking is not why so few women regret their abortions, but why anyone ever assumed they would. The assumption rests on a patronizing fiction: that women do not truly understand what they are choosing, that their emotions will eventually catch up to reveal a hidden wound. Decades of evidence have dismantled that fiction completely. Women know what they are doing. They know why they are doing it. And overwhelmingly, when asked months and years later, they stand by the decision with a quiet, unshakable resolve that no political campaign has managed to undo. If we are genuinely interested in women's well-being — rather than in controlling their choices — we owe it to them to stop projecting regret onto experiences that, for the vast majority, carried none.
